definition: One's range of knowledge or sight.
origin: From Middle English kennen, from Old English cennan (to make known, declare), from Proto-Germanic kannjan.

rounds.label: Round 1 of 3 · Etymology
rounds.context: Where does Ken come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: The boundary of what is known

rounds.label: Round 2 of 3 · Definition
rounds.context: What does Ken mean? Match each clue to the right answer.

pairs.b: Refers to things we cannot comprehend

rounds.label: Round 3 of 3 · Usage
rounds.context: How is Ken used? Match each fragment to its correct completion.

pairs.b: Beyond the ken of primary pupils
The horizon, just within our ken

definition: The central or most important part of something, or the edible seed within a nut casing.
origin: From Old English cyrnel, a diminutive of corn (grain, seed).

rounds.label: Round 1 of 3 · Etymology
rounds.context: Where does Kernel come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: A tiny grain or inner core
A common cereal crop plant grain

rounds.label: Round 2 of 3 · Definition
rounds.context: What does Kernel mean? Match each clue to the right answer.

pairs.b: Manages core communication with computer hardware

rounds.label: Round 3 of 3 · Usage
rounds.context: How is Kernel used? Match each fragment to its correct completion.

pairs.b: Kernel of the digital audio software
Kernel of their entire business strategy

definition: To arouse or inspire an emotion, or to light a fire.
origin: From Middle English kindlen, from Old Norse kynda (to light a fire, inflame).

rounds.label: Round 1 of 3 · Etymology
rounds.context: Where does Kindle come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: To set on fire or spark
Dry material used to catch a spark

rounds.label: Round 2 of 3 · Definition
rounds.context: What does Kindle mean? Match each clue to the right answer.

pairs.b: Makes learning easy for primary school children

rounds.label: Round 3 of 3 · Usage
rounds.context: How is Kindle used? Match each fragment to its correct completion.

pairs.b: A deep creative interest in Year 4
Kindle the campfire down the beach
Kindle a sudden spark of resentment

definition: A sharing of characteristics or origins, or blood relationship.
origin: From Old English cynn (family, race, nature) + the suffix -ship (state, condition).

rounds.label: Round 1 of 3 · Etymology
rounds.context: Where does Kinship come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: The state of belonging to a family

rounds.label: Round 2 of 3 · Definition
rounds.context: What does Kinship mean? Match each clue to the right answer.

pairs.b: Provides essential support in small villages

rounds.label: Round 3 of 3 · Usage

pairs.b: Kinship upon starting the software project
Of structural kinship within the club

origin: From Turkish kısmet, from Arabic qismah (portion, lot, division, fate).

rounds.context: Where does Kismet come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: The separation of a whole into parts

pairs.b: Implies humans have no real control over life

definition: The sound of a bell, especially when rung solemnly for a death or funeral.
origin: From Old English cnyll (sound of a bell), from cnyllan (to strike sound out, ring).

rounds.label: Round 1 of 3 · Etymology
rounds.context: Where does Knell come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: A tolling sound or signal stroke
To clatter or make a loud din
A solemn bell strike or chime
An old word for standard bell ringing

rounds.label: Round 2 of 3 · Definition
rounds.context: What does Knell mean? Match each clue to the right answer.

pairs.b: An omen of death, termination, or ruin
Signals the definitive end of an era or system

rounds.label: Round 3 of 3 · Usage
rounds.context: How is Knell used? Match each fragment to its correct completion.

pairs.b: Death knell for the local school library
Echo across the churchyard at twilight
Knell for traditional print publishing houses
Sad knell for our cup tournament dreams

definition: To act in an excessively subservient or sycophantic manner.
origin: From Chinese koutou, from kou (knock) + tou (head).

rounds.label: Round 1 of 3 · Etymology
rounds.context: Where does Kowtow come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: The custom of touching the forehead to the ground in respect
To bow down low or grovel submissively

rounds.label: Round 2 of 3 · Definition
rounds.context: What does Kowtow mean? Match each clue to the right answer.

pairs.b: To fawn, grovel, or behave obsequiously
Shows strong independent strength of character

rounds.label: Round 3 of 3 · Usage
rounds.context: How is Kowtow used? Match each fragment to its correct completion.

pairs.b: The ridiculous demands of the executive board
To the manager just for promotions
Never kowtow to high-handed school bureaucrats
